title: Colorado Federal Judge: ICE Agents Admit Incomplete Training, Warrants 'Riddled With Mistakes'
excerpt: During a federal hearing in Denver, U.S. government attorneys acknowledged that ICE agents are 'not fully trained' and that field warrants were 'riddled with mistakes and inconsistencies.' Judge R. Brooke Jackson stated he could not assume agents were 'getting trained' given that he had not seen a single properly filled-out warrant.
content:
On March 11, 2026, during a hearing before U.S. District Court Senior Judge R. Brooke Jackson in Denver, Assistant U.S. attorneys made a striking admission about the state of ICE officer training and compliance.

Government lawyers acknowledged that ICE agents in Colorado and nationally 'aren't fully trained compared to their predecessors' and that warrants filled out in the field 'are riddled with mistakes and inconsistencies, and don't comply with federal law.' They also acknowledged that warrantless arrests were 'still happening' despite a preliminary injunction.

'I can't assume these guys are getting trained or understand when we haven't seen one [arrest warrant] that was filled out properly.' — Judge R. Brooke Jackson
  • The hearing arose from a November 2025 injunction Jackson had issued finding ICE's warrantless arrests unlawful.
  • The judge noted that Washington, D.C. had been 'giving conflicting guidance' about the legality of warrantless arrests as recently as January 2026.
  • Jackson stated he was expected to rule on whether DHS was in violation of his order and what remedies to impose.
mediaPlaceholder: Court transcript excerpts showing government attorneys acknowledging ICE training deficiencies
imageAlt: Federal judge hears ICE agents’ confusion about law, injunction on warrantless arrests
